user_prompt: also, for the button types that are not GPIO, as in, cannot have an ISR, I still want the poll function to record the down time and such
agent_reply: Updated [Buttons.cpp](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Buttons/Buttons.cpp) and [Buttons.h](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Buttons/Buttons.h) to behave more like the old reference code.
Key changes:
- Added old-style debounce using `BTN_DEBOUNCE`, with a local default if it is not defined.
- Added `clr_time`, volatile press/down counters, and clear-time behavior like the reference.
- GPIO buttons now attach an object-aware ISR via `attachInterruptArg`, so the class-owned `press_cnt` is updated by the interrupt.
- GPIO `poll()` now clears `down_time` on release and can optionally catch missed ISR edges under `TRY_CATCH_MISSED_GPIO_ISR`.
- Touch and power buttons still use `Button::poll()`, so their `down_time`, held time, and press counters are recorded even though they cannot use ISR.
- App handlers for ISR-backed GPIO buttons are dispatched later from `poll()`, not inside the ISR.
I still couldn’t run a PlatformIO build because `pio`/`platformio` isn’t available in this shell.

Implemented `Hotel` as a namespace-backed power manager in [Hotel.h](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Hotel/Hotel.h) and [Hotel.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Hotel/Hotel.cpp).
What’s included:
- State machine from the README: blocked, active thresholds, light sleep states, shutdown.
- `pollCore0()` / `pollCore1()` synchronization before light sleep.
- User activity API, including ISR-safe activity notification.
- Blocking while recording, Bluetooth is active, Wi-Fi is active, or audio mode is active.
- Transition-only CPU/backlight changes with debug transition logging.
- `esp_timer_get_time()` wrapper for sleep-safe elapsed time.
- M5 PMIC shutdown, M5 light sleep when ready, timer-only ESP light sleep fallback before `M5.begin()`.
I also wired it into:
- [main.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp): both core loops call Hotel polling.
- [Buttons.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Buttons/Buttons.cpp): button activity resets Hotel timer.
- [FlyGui.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp): touch activity resets Hotel timer.

Verification: `pio run` still fails before compiling `Hotel` because of the existing `SdCard` namespace collision with SdFat in `src/mic_sd_recorder_demo.cpp`. The error is the same prior blocker: repo `namespace SdCard` conflicts with SdFat’s global `SdCard` typedef.

user_prompt: identify any place where actual ISRs are used
reason: documentation says we can save IRAM by
Enable CONFIG_FREERTOS_PLACE_FUNCTIONS_INTO_FLASH. Provided these functions are not incorrectly used from ISRs, this option is safe to enable in all configurations.
Enable CONFIG_RINGBUF_PLACE_FUNCTIONS_INTO_FLASH. Provided these functions are not incorrectly used from ISRs, this option is safe to enable in all configurations.
Enable CONFIG_RINGBUF_PLACE_ISR_FUNCTIONS_INTO_FLASH. This option is not safe to use if the ISR ringbuf functions are used from an IRAM interrupt context, e.g., if
agent_reply: I checked without changing code.
**Actual ISR Use**
- [Buttons.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Buttons/Buttons.cpp:18): real GPIO ISR, `IRAM_ATTR gpioButtonIsr`, registered with `attachInterruptArg`.

- [Buttons.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Buttons/Buttons.cpp:151): ISR path calls `GpioButton::handleInterrupt()`, which calls `Hotel::noteUserActivityFromIsr()`, `millis()`, and an optional user ISR callback.
- [Hotel.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Hotel/Hotel.cpp:293): `noteUserActivityFromIsr()` uses `portENTER_CRITICAL_ISR` / `portEXIT_CRITICAL_ISR`.
- [AudioManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:473): I2S event callbacks are marked `IRAM_ATTR`.
- [AudioManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:538): those callbacks are registered with `i2s_channel_register_event_callback`.
There is also `lib/Buttons/Buttons_old_code_for_reference.ino` with old ISR examples, but that looks like reference code, not the active implementation.
**Ringbuffer Use**
I did not find project-owned usage of ESP-IDF ringbuffers: no `xRingbuffer*`, `vRingbuffer*`, or `freertos/ringbuf.h` in `src`, `lib`, or `inc`.
But ESP-IDF itself has ISR ringbuffer users:
- [i2c.c](</c:/Users/frank/.platformio/packages/framework-espidf/components/driver/i2c/i2c.c:611>) uses `xRingbufferSendFromISR` and related ISR ringbuffer calls.
- deprecated RMT and ADC DMA drivers also use ringbuffer ISR functions.
- Bluetooth SPP/L2CAP use ringbuffers, mostly non-ISR paths.
**Config Risk**

`CONFIG_FREERTOS_PLACE_FUNCTIONS_INTO_FLASH` looks risky for this project right now. The ESP-IDF I2S driver has IRAM DMA callbacks that call FreeRTOS `FromISR` queue APIs, and your code also has ISR critical sections. I would not enable this blindly.

`CONFIG_RINGBUF_PLACE_FUNCTIONS_INTO_FLASH` is probably lower risk because your code does not directly use ESP-IDF ringbuffers from normal task context. This may be okay, especially if SPP/L2CAP are disabled.

`CONFIG_RINGBUF_PLACE_ISR_FUNCTIONS_INTO_FLASH` is the one I would treat as unsafe until proven otherwise. Even though your code does not call ISR ringbuffer APIs, ESP-IDF I2C/RMT/ADC code does. Since this project uses I2C through M5/Arduino/device drivers, I would assume this needs caution.

user_prompt: we are polling the PMIC pretty frequently, right? are we getting the power button pressed state but just not using it?
agent_reply: Yes, we poll PMIC-ish state pretty frequently, but we are not using the power button state.
What’s happening:
- [BattTracker::poll()](/D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BattTracker/BattTracker.cpp:47) runs every `1000 ms`.
- Each update reads:
- `M5.Power.getBatteryVoltage()`
- `M5.Power.isCharging()`
- USB/AC present via `M5.Power.getType()` then AXP-specific calls like `M5.Power.Axp192.isACIN()`
- The GUI also calls `M5.update()` in [FlyGui::poll()](/D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:144), usually much more often than once per second unless slowed by audio/Wi-Fi mode.
Power button state exists in the codebase here:
```cpp
bool PwrButton::isPressed()
return M5.BtnPWR.isPressed();
in [Buttons.cpp](/D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Buttons/Buttons.cpp:252).

But: I don’t see any `PwrButton` instance being constructed, and the GUI dispatch loop only checks `TOUCHBUTTON_LEFT`, `TOUCHBUTTON_CENTER`, and `TOUCHBUTTON_RIGHT` in [FlyGui.cpp](/D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:267). So the wrapper exists, but the app is not wiring it into the button array or dispatching it.
So the answer is basically:
- `M5.update()` is probably updating `M5.BtnPWR`.
- `BattTracker` is polling PMIC power/battery data, but not the power button.
- We have a `PwrButton` class ready to read `M5.BtnPWR.isPressed()`.
- We are not instantiating or using it anywhere.

A straightforward path would be either add a `PwrButton` instance and dispatch it, or more simply check `M5.BtnPWR.wasPressed()` / held duration in `FlyGui::dispatchButtons()` or a top-level poll path.
